column1: # CATS Priority Group Completion Analysis
# This experiment demonstrates CATS queue jumping by sending
# priority groups in REVERSE order (P4 first, P0 last).
# CATS should reorder delivery so P0, P1 arrive first, then P2, P3, and P4 last, while respecting fairness.
# SENDING ORDER (worst-case for baseline TCP):
# 1st: P4 Analytics/Tracking (150KB) - LARGEST, lowest priority
# 2nd: P3 Images/Media (60KB) - large
# 3rd: P2 Application JS (40KB) - medium
# 4th: P1 CSS Framework (25KB) - small
# 5th: P0 Critical HTML/CSS (8KB) - SMALLEST, highest priority
# EXPECTED CATS DELIVERY ORDER:
# P0, P1 complete first despite being sent last
# P2, P3 complete in priority order
# P4 completes last despite being sent first
# This demonstrates CATS's core value proposition:
# Critical content gets priority regardless of send order
# TSV FILE FORMAT AND INTERPRETATION GUIDE:
# Columns:
# 1. SimTime(s)        - Absolute simulation time when group completed
# 2. PageID           - Page identifier (always 0 in single-page experiment)
# 3. Priority         - CATS priority level (0=highest, 4=lowest)
# 4. CompletionTime(ms) - Relative completion time from first byte received
# 5. Event            - Always 'GROUP_COMPLETED' for this experiment
# HOW TO INTERPRET THE RESULTS:
# - Lower CompletionTime(ms) = Faster delivery
# - Priority 0 should have lowest CompletionTime (fastest)
# - Priority 4 should have highest CompletionTime (slowest)
# - CATS Success: P0 < P1 < P2 < P3 < P4 completion times
# - TCP Without CATS: P4 < P3 < P2 < P1 < P0 (send order)
# Format: SimTime(s)
